data.title: REITs: Income Goldmine Or Value Pitfall?
data.description:
REITs are one of the fundamental cornerstones for most income-based strategies and portfolios. They embody the necessary characteristics that support durable current income, inflation protection and gradual price appreciation.

I like to label REITs as quasi-equity instruments. With a quasi-equity I mean a combination of bond-like and equity-like features.

The bond element comes through the following items:

  • Long-term lease agreements with relatively fixed prices (similar to coupons).
  • Relatively minor organic growth opportunities due to the requirement to distribute the lion's share of earnings in dividends.
  • Generally, protected asset base with quite low business risk.

The equity element kicks in through the following items:

  • Leases tied to annual fixed rate or CPI-linked escalators.
  • The ability to secure some growth by reinvesting the undistributed cash amount, or cash that is collected by selling properties at a higher price than what was initially invested.
  • The ability to issue fresh equity that could be combined with an additional leverage to expand the portfolio in an accretive fashion.

The bond factors render the valuations relatively low, which, in turn, help drive up the dividend yields.

The equity factors bring in the dividend inflation protection aspect as well as the potential for a gradual price appreciation return component.

Again, if we put these things together, I think you will agree with me that we arrive at an investment product that fits very well into most income based strategies/portfolios.

Having said that, there is a catch.

And the catch is that nobody wants to have high exposure to an asset base that systematically underperforms other income alternatives. And the situation becomes even worse if the dividends themselves do not match or exceed CPI (inflation) levels in the economy on the ground.

Since 2022, REITs could be put into that category.

Namely, since interest rates started to go up, the REITs followed, but just in the opposite direction.

Now, if we turn back to the quasi-equity story and zoom into the bond-like features, we will have to conclude that all of this makes sense. Bonds are high duration assets that are negatively correlated to the interest rates. The higher the yields, the lower the bond prices. The same applies to REITs. For some more, for some less, but, in general, it is relevant for the lion's share of the REIT players - hence, the drop in the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VNQ).

The question now is what the direction of travel will be going forward.

Could it be so that REITs will continue to remain depressed and underperform income-producing alternatives such as MLPs, dividend blue-chip corporates and various CEFs? Or is this actually an opportunity to invest heavily before the recovery takes place?

Let me discuss this in the next section.

REITs: Trap or opportunity?

I know that some of you will not be happy about this answer, but my response is that we really do not know. The reason for this is that it all depends on the systematic factors that determine the pathway for long-term yields.

Having said that, I think that it is possible though to understand which scenario is more likely than the other (i.e., REITs going up or REITs continuing to underperform).

In my humble opinion, the pessimistic scenario is more likely.

I give you three reasons for this.

First, even though REITs have experienced a decent contraction in their valuation multiples, which, theoretically, should imply an interesting opportunity, in reality, the entry point is not that attractive.

What really drives REIT valuations are the levels and its changes in the long-term yield space. This is why, for example, the recent base rate cuts have not moved the needle for REIT valuations. And if we compare REIT yields with the long-term "risk-free" yields, then the situation looks rather bad. The spread is negative. To get the spread back to the historical norm, one of the following things has to happen:

  • Long-term yields going down.
  • REIT dividend yields going up.

Second, and this is where I think the rubber hits the road. In my view, the likelihood of the long-term yields going down any time soon is extremely low. Let's take a look at what happens with the fiscal deficits, geopolitics, foreign capital outflows from the USD assets etc. These are items that are inherently supportive for having high and even rising long-term yields. So, the only realistic option that remains is REITs getting repriced lower to warrant a healthy and positive spread from the U.S. Treasury yields.

Third, we should not rule out an increasing inflation rate. The presence of notable tariffs, de-globalization, production reshoring from low-cost regions etc., are all inflationary items. Granted, so far, we have not seen material effects from the newly introduced tariffs, but we should definitely be cognizant of lagged-effects. In case the inflation level goes up, the chances are quite high that the base rates will not get reduced to, say, ~2% level. I know that a long-term yield level is what matters for REITs, but having a friendly base rate at least allows saving on interest costs through credit facilities and shorter term debt instruments. Plus, higher inflation will then make the real (not nominal) yield spread between REIT yields and the U.S. Treasury yield even less attractive for investors.

Investor action items

In a nutshell, I do not see a sector-wide bullish case for REITs any time soon.

However, it does not mean that there are no opportunities at all in this space.

Given the macro picture that I outlined above, I see the following investment plays that REIT investors could explore:

1. Real estate closed-end fund instruments: In case the base rates fall and the inflation risk does not materialize (or at least to such an extent that would keep the Fed away from cutting the rates), real estate CEF funds could deliver very strong returns. Since CEF vehicles tend to assume leverage through short-term debt instruments to magnify their overall portfolio yields, then the situation in which the base rates fall, but the asset yields remain stable or even rise could lead to an enhanced spread capture. Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred Income Fund (NYSE:RNP) is a great example with short-term debt focused leverage and exposure to relatively high-yielding and solid quality REITs.

2. Competitive cost of debt REITs: At its core, the risk of high long-term yields is about unfavorable cost of debt. It is clear that there are no mechanisms for REITs to mitigate the long-term yield risk against which their bonds/borrowings are priced. Yet, what they can do is to keep the credit risk premiums as low as possible. The way to do that is through fortress balance sheets and upper investment grade credit ratings. In this case, Realty Income Corporation (NYSE:O) is a REIT to consider (i.e., one of the few REITs with upper investment grade balance sheets), especially given how low the multiple has become, at ~P/FFO 13.1x, while the 10-year historical average is ~17.0x.

3. Screaming bargains: One can never go wrong with picking up securities that trade way below their intrinsic values. For example, in my view, this is the case for Alexandria Real Estate Equities Inc. (NYSE:ARE), which also has an upper investment grade balance sheet, ~60% FFO payout, resilient portfolio and positive lease spread statistics. However, largely due to the market fears of pharma and biotech companies reducing their R&D spend levels, ARE's stock has been completely hammered. The P/FFO has dropped to 7.6x and the dividend has jumped to 7.5% (well over the U.S. Treasury yield). Please see more details on this case in my recent ARE-specific article: Alexandria Real Estate: The True Definition Of A Dividend Bargain.

data.title: First Industrial Realty: Attractive Despite Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
Shares of First Industrial Realty Trust (NYSE:FR) have been a modest performer over the past year, gaining just about 3%. As a major owner of logistics real estate with a significant West Coast presence, FR has meaningful exposure to tariffs, particularly on China. That is why shares sold off sharply in early April and have not fully recovered. I last covered shares in November 2024, rating the stock a "buy;" however, initial tariff actions were harsher than I expected. As a result, the stock has lost about 8% of its value. Given this new backdrop, now is a good time to see if FR can recover or if investors should sell.

First Industrial is a leading owner of warehouse and logistic assets across the country. As you can see, it has assets across the United States, though most of its assets are located in coastal markets. That is because warehouses are disproportionately built around ports, as there needs to be a place to store imported goods after they arrive before being distributed across the country. While over the long-term this is an advantageous asset base, there are clearly near-term headwinds. I would note that given its large and diverse footprint, its top 20 tenants are only 26% of base rent.

Increased tariffs are likely to reduce imports, all else equal. Indeed, as you can see below, imports plunged in April after tariffs took effect. Of course, imports were especially high in March, as companies sought to front-run these tariffs. Given the time it takes goods to reach U.S. shores, I expect further weakness to be reported in May. However, with President Trump pulling back tariffs across nations, there will likely be some rebound in June and onwards.

The challenge is that we do not know what the ultimate tariff policy is likely to be. At this point, I assume at least a 10% baseline tariff will persist, and it seems likely that China will face higher duties than other countries, though even there the Administration has taken a more conciliatory approach. FR is highly exposed to activity near West Coast ports. Now, even if tariffs on China are higher, if that production is moved to Vietnam or India, FR should see little pressure. To the extent production is rerouted to Mexico or onshored to the U.S., it will likely be a headwind for FR, as its warehouses would no longer be near where these goods are made/arrive.

At the moment, I believe businesses are frozen. Without knowing what tariff rates will be, they are highly unlikely to make long-term commitments. Policy is so uncertain, and frequently changing, that I expect this paralysis to persist. Additionally, with tariffs implemented by executive order, the next President may simply remove them if they remain in place. If tariffs only last 3 years, we are unlikely to see a major shift to U.S. production, as it can take that long to even build a factory.

One other potential risk is that FR could see a decline in trade activity lead its customers to go bankrupt and default on their leases. It has a fairly modest $1 million set aside for bad debt expense, though even in COVID, losses were under $2 million. Importantly, FR has less than 500k square feet to China third-party logistics providers. It has largely turned down these firms for failing to meet its stringent credit requirements. It also has insignificant auto manufacturing exposure.

While policy has been surprising, at a macro level, I do not see the current tariff policy as likely to cause material changes in trade flows. Still, import volume is likely to be somewhat lower over the next twelve months, given higher prices, which could reduce consumer demand. Ultimately, I see this as a manageable headwind for the business. Unsurprisingly given how much uncertainty there is, FR is seeing conversations with tenants taking longer as customers were slowing discussions about new leases, pending the resolution of tariffs, as disclosed on the most recent earnings call.

I expect this tepid demand environment to persist for at least six months. Accordingly, management is seeing better demand for small spaces than large ones, where there is a lower commitment. Additionally, I can envision a scenario where tenants seek shorter leases. With more policy uncertainty, businesses may be comfortable with a 12 or 24 month lease, but not a 60-month lease.

While the demand side is likely weakening, there should be some offset by an improving supply side. There was a wave of construction in the wake of COVID, given very low interest rates and a surge in goods consumption. As consumption flat-lined and interest rates rose, construction of new warehouses became less economical. We are now through the worst of the completion activity, and new supply going forward should be fairly modest, at least through early 2027. Lower supply should help FR maintain occupancy and pricing power, even in a somewhat slower demand environment.

These factors can be seen in its recent financials. In the company's first quarter reported on April 16th, First Industrial generated $0.68 of FFO, which was $0.02 below expectations, even as revenue grew by 9% to $177 million. G&A rose by $4 million, a surprisingly large increase. There was nonrecurring accelerated share-based compensation, which drove this. It still expects full year G&A to be below $42 million, and costs should come back down in Q2. Adjusted EBITDA rose by 9% to $114 million, and property expenses were up just 3% from last year.

Despite the headwinds described above, FR generated 10.1% same-store net operating growth. This is a strong level, though I expect it to decelerate as the trade uncertainty drags, and year-over-year comparisons become more difficult. FR had 95.3% occupancy in Q1, down 90bps sequentially and 20bps from a year ago. Leasing activity does remain high, with new leases signed at a 30% premium from prior leases. As you can see below, the strength of the business has clearly moderated from its peak, but it continues to run at a reasonable level.

Looking forward, FR has 2 million square feet under construction, focused on the Sun Belt at a $280 million cost. So far, these projects are just 43% leased and will come online over the next nine months. It expects to have 1.5 million sq feet leased in Q4, though I see some risk it does take longer to fill this space. If uncertainty persists, I expect FR to reduce its go-forward development activity, after completing these in-the-works projects. On the positive side, FR leased the remaining 50% of its new Denver facility, pointing to ongoing demand in less trade-sensitive areas. With newbuild activity focused away from the West Coast, there should be less sensitivity to China and Asian imports.

FR also maintains a strong balance sheet. It has no maturities until 2027 on its $2.4 billion of debt, which leaves it well-insulated from interest rates. While leverage ticked up in Q1, it will come back to 4.9x as projects come online and begin contributing EBITDA, leaving leverage consistent with the low end of historic ranges.

Given this strong balance sheet, FR increased capital returns more aggressively than even I expected. In February, it raised its dividend a surprisingly strong 20% to $0.445, giving shares a 3.6% yield. At the midpoint of its guidance, shares still have a 1.6x coverage ratio. Last year, I wrote that FR could increase its dividend faster than FFO, given excess coverage, but management closed the gap more aggressively than expected. Still, FR retains over $125 million of cash flow, more than enough to meet development needs while maintaining leverage.

Guidance for $2.92 of FFO is actually higher than my $2.80 forecast from November, reflecting strong leasing at the end of last year. I do see a risk that it takes longer to lease its development pipeline, though this is unlikely to be more than a $0.05 risk. As such, I see about $2.80-$2.90 of FFO this year. Even as FFO and its dividend hike have exceeded my expectations, shares are down since November, speaking to how much negativity there is.

Frankly, I think this is an opportunity, especially as current tariff leaves are unlikely to dramatically alter trade flows, in my view. I also view a ~1.4x coverage ratio as sustainable for this business, meaning we are likely to see ongoing dividend hikes in future years. This gives shares of ~$2.00 dividend capacity, or just over a 4% yield. This dividend cushion, alongside ~5-6% underlying FFO growth, supports 6-10% dividend increases for several years to come. Combined with a starting yield of 3.6%, that points to a low double-digit total return. This makes FR an attractive buy as macro fears are outsized relative to fundamental performance.

data.title: AMDL - A Useful Speculative Tool, Not A Buy And Hold
data.description:
To grasp the construction is to grasp the use

Mankind has been building all sorts of tools for millennia now. But we do have to be careful to use the right tool for the job - both copper furnaces and frying pans are great gadgets but we do have to distinguish in their uses.

The same is true of financial instruments - they are, after all, tools designed to give certain outcomes. It helps if we understand how and why a tool is constructed so that we can then use it in the right way. Or, more importantly, don't end up using it the wrong way and so lose our hard earned money. Lose our money by simply not knowing what we're doing that is.

This particular instrument under discussion here is for speculation or hedging - not for a long tem buy and hold. So, that's the way we should use it as well.

GraniteShares 2x Long AMD Daily ETF (NASDAQ:AMDL)

What the fund intends to do is pretty much there in the title. It's from Granite, it's a leveraged - to two times - attempt to follow the long performance of AMD stock. Or, as the ETF itself puts it:

The Fund seeks daily investment results, before fees andexpenses, of 2 times (200%) the daily percentage changeof the common stock of Advanced Micro Devices, Inc.(NASDAQ: AMD)

Note the daily performance there. Note also this from the fund themselves:

The Fund seeks daily leveraged investment results and are intended to be used as short-term trading vehicles. The Fund is very different from most mutual funds and exchange-traded funds.

When the guy offering for sale is insisting this is a short term trading vehicle probably wise of us to believe him.

OK, so why should we believe him?

One problem is that "daily". This leads to a daily price reset and thus:

For example, consider the example of a 3x leveraged ETF where the benchmark rises from 100 to 102 (+2%) on day-1, and falls from 102 back to 100 (-1.96%) on day-2. The benchmark index is back to its original level of 100, so has flat performance.

For the leveraged ETF, however, the return would be negative. To illustrate, after the first day of trading, an initial $100 investment on a 3x leveraged ETF would rise to $106.

$100 initial + (($100 x 2% index return) x 3) = $106

The next day the 3x leveraged ETF would drop 5.88% (3 x -1.96%), resulting in a value of only $99.77.

$106 - 5.88% = $99.77

Even though the benchmark index was flat, the leveraged ETF would have resulted in a loss of -0.23% over these 2 days.

$99.77 - $100 initial value = $-0.23

We also get the SEC making much the same point:

Leveraged and inverse ETFs typically are designed to achieve their stated performance objectives on a daily basis. Some investors might invest in these ETFs with the expectation that the ETFs may meet their stated daily performance objectives over the long term as well. Investors should be aware that performance of these ETFs over a period longer than one day can differ significantly from their stated daily performance objectives and may potentially expose investors to significant and sudden losses.

The point about daily resets is compounded. For the way that the leverage is achieved that the fund buys derivatives. The point about derivatives is that they have a time value - a part of the value of a derivative is how long it lasts for. So, the passage of time, by definition, reduces the value of the derivative. This is true whatever form of derivative is used. Thus using derivatives over longer periods of time is guaranteeing a loss of value - the loss of time value of the derivative.

As the fund says:

Because of daily rebalancing and the compounding of each day’s return over time, the return of the Fund for periods longer than a singleday will be the result of each day’s returns compounded over the period, which will very likely differ from 200% of the return of theUnderlying Stock over the same period. The Fund will lose money if the Underlying Stock’s performance is flat over time,

This isn't something to hold.

Finances

This is an open ended fund so, in theory, could be subject to a redemption cascade - we all turn up asking for our money at the same time. They then have to sell the fund's assets at fire sale prices and disaster. As they're in derivatives fixing prices for a day the chance of this is so remote as to be non-existent.

As an open ended fun they're not going to stray more than mere basis points from net asset value either.

Daily volume's around the 20 million pieces level - we'll have no problem moving in and out in size. The spread's a penny - we can indeed move in size.

The management fee's 0.99%, total costs some 1.15%. Those are annual numbers, of course, so the effect of them on a - maximum - few days holding is not going to be large.

We can check this hold and lose idea.

AMDL:

AMDS:

Two times the 1x short should equal one times the 2x long. They should balance each other - and at the short end they largely do (minus a little bit in fees). Over the year there we can see that they don't, not at all. That's the long term degradation in performance given by leveraged ETFs.

So, what is AMDL for?

This is for speculation - or hedging. It's wholly possible that we might want to take a day trade on AMD stock. Doing so in a leveraged ETF gives us a cheap way of doing that - for the day, as the construction of the instrument is all about.

The other use is hedging. Say that we think - on a particular day - that AMD will outperform the S&P 500. So, we go short (probably via another ETF) the S&P and long AMD. That locks in the relative performance of AMD as against the large cap market. There are ETFs that will enable us to do this against the computing stocks, electric and so on. None of these are trading recommendations, they're simply examples.

It's impossible for me, here, to say that such speculation or hedging should be done on any particular day. Simply because it's impossible to know, ahead of time, the conditions that might make such a speculation worthwhile.

All that can be said is that AMDL is a perfectly good tool to use in such circumstances. It's designed to be so used, it's well designed to be so used. But it is to be only so used. This is a short term speculative or hedging tool, not a long term buy and hold.

Why I'm wrong

In the design and use of the tool I'm not. As to whether the tool should actually be used that's a matter of taste. Mine isn't for short term speculations but others differ.

My opinion

If we assume that the intent is short term speculation - or a hedge against just such another speculation - then AMDL is a perfectly fine instrument. But it is that, as Seeking Alpha, the SEC, Granite and I all tell you. It is not for a long term buy and hold, an investment. So, don't use it that way.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Undervalued And Oversold
data.description:
My thesis

Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B) stock demonstrated price decline after Warren Buffett announced that he is retiring soon. Nevertheless, this safe harbor is still notably ahead of the broader equity market, with a 5.6% gain since early February. This means that my old bullish call aged well. I see no reason to switch to something other than Strong Buy because this stock is a great defensive play amid the current extremely uncertain environment. Despite some relief in President Trump’s trade wars, most of the trade deals with the largest partners are just temporary and recent rhetoric has not been that dovish. The valuation of Berkshire Hathaway is still quite attractive as well.

Berkshire Hathaway stock analysis

I start with the latest available quarterly financials, a crucial development for this widely diversified conglomerate. The company’s Q1 consolidated revenue of $89.7 billion was quite stable on a YoY basis, just slightly below the same quarter of FY2024. There was a notable decline in the company’s net operating earnings, though, a 14% decrease to $9.6 billion.

This was mostly explained by one-off events rather than some fundamental problems, as the insurance business saw a notable dip in underwriting income due to $1.1 billion in catastrophe losses from the California wildfires. This loss was partially offset by the improved investment income, from around $2.6 billion to $2.9 billion.

Another large segment that demonstrated earnings decline was “Manufacturing, service and retailing”. But the decline was immaterial, around $28 million for a $3 billion total. On the other hand, railroad earnings were up by a solid 6% YoY. Berkshire Hathaway Energy earnings also demonstrated strong growth, from $717 million to around $1.1 billion. As we can see, Berkshire’s operating segments demonstrate strength and ability to drive more value for shareholders. I am not mentioning the notable investment loss in Q1 because one quarter is just nothing for a company that has kept its investments for decades and has a very cautious approach to investing. The important part is that the company is in a massive $347 billion cash and cash equivalents position (including short-term securities), which can be used to invest in new promising names.

I am confident that this $347 billion cash pile will be used to create more value for shareholders because of the company’s stellar proven record of success. Warren Buffett is an investing legend who was able to beat the broader equity market for decades. Yes, he is retiring soon, but we already know the successor, Greg Abel. It is not a spontaneous decision because this succession plan was initially mentioned by Berkshire’s leadership four years ago. Therefore, Greg Abel had plenty of time to get prepared for the role. Moreover, he has been part of the conglomerate since 1999 when CalEnergy became part of Berkshire Hathaway and highly likely has been working very closely with Warren Buffett and Charlie Munger since 2018 when he joined the company’s board of directors. Therefore, his experience and the adherence to Berkshire’s values should not be a concern.

Besides company-specific positives, the environment also shows us various signs that betting on a high-quality safe harbor like Berkshire Hathaway is a reasonable move. There is still vast uncertainty related to the Fed’s next moves in terms of the monetary policy. The Fed remains quite hawkish and there were still no rate cuts this year despite less than a month left before the first half of the year ends. Higher interest rates for longer will certainly weigh on investors’ confidence in long-duration assets, making a defensive play like Berkshire Hathaway compelling.

The soaring gold price is another large sign of investors seeking safe assets. After some trade wars lull a few weeks ago, President Trump demonstrates some signs of returning to a more hawkish rhetoric with his recent words that China violated the temporary trade deal. In addition, there was an order signed regarding a large 50% tariffs increase on steel and aluminum. Therefore, the tariffs agenda does not look completely off the table.

Last but not least, the technical setup looks extremely good. The stock's current RSI indicator of around 35 is low, meaning that BRK.B is oversold. Berkshire's RSI indicator is rarely dips that low, which looks like an apparent bullish signal as well.

Intrinsic value calculation

Berkshire’s robust YTD performance helps the company to maintain position in the $1 trillion market cap club. I think it is fair that Berkshire’s valuation ratios are above the industry median because it is absolutely unparalleled in terms of the quality of management and scale. In addition, there is unmatched profitability that justifies higher multiples. The stellar track record of Warren Buffett against the S&P 500 since the 1960s is another crucial factor that makes Berkshire unique.

I believe that assessing Berkshire's valuation attractiveness through comparative analysis of its current multiples against historical ones is a sound approach. Based on this analysis, the stock is fairly valued because valuation ratios are very close to historical averages. This is very good because a company like Berkshire deserves a premium over the fair value.

Furthermore, Wall Street analysts covering Berkshire suggest that the stock is undervalued by around 6% because of the consensus target price of $521. The trend presented above suggests that the stock has been historically quite predictable in closing the gap between its actual share price and consensus target price. This reinforces the assertion that the stock is currently attractively valued.

What can go wrong with my thesis?

Investors are currently much more tolerant of taking risks than they were in March and April. This increases demand for riskier assets like growth stocks, meaning that safer harbors like Berkshire might lack attention from investors. I think that such a pivot in the stock market sentiment might be another reason (apart from the announcement that Warren Buffett retires next year) for the share price underperformance over the last month.

Another reason why Berkshire’s share price performance was weak in May is seasonality. This one has been historically the weakest month for this stock, with a mere 36.4% win rate. We also have to warn readers that June has not been historically stronger, with a slightly higher win rate of 40%. Therefore, the recent share price weakness might continue during the first month of Summer.

Summary

There are no reasons to change Berkshire Hathaway’s rating to something else other than Strong Buy. The fundamentals of this unparalleled safe harbor are rock-solid, and the stock is very attractively valued.
